prompt: Black and white photograph in the style of Soviet Constructivist photography, 1920s–1930s, in the manner of Alexander Rodchenko. Extreme low-angle shot (rakurs) looking up at a lone figure descending a steep mountain slope. The figure is shot from below and to the side — their silhouette is bold and graphic against a sky of dense, churning fog. They lean decisively into the descent, a long wooden staff planted firmly on the immediate slope ahead of them, body weight committed forward and downward. The composition is strongly diagonal — the mountain slope cuts across the frame at 40 degrees, the figure's body echoing the angle. The immediate terrain is sharply rendered in high contrast — deep black shadows in the rock crevices, bright highlights on the slope surface directly underfoot. Above and beyond the figure, the fog is total and opaque, erasing any view of the wider landscape. At the far upper corner of the frame, a single break in the fog reveals a glimpse of a vast deeper valley below — rendered as a bright graphic shape, clearly visible to the viewer, clearly beyond the figure's field of vision. High contrast black and white. Hard directional light. Graphic, flat, monumental. No soft gradients, no warm tones, no naturalistic depth rendering. No diagrams, no graphs, no text, no labels, no mathematical notation, no equations, no floating annotations, no non-physical elements, no neural network visualisations, no data flow arrows, no UI elements, no screen displays, no infographic elements. Everything in the scene must be a physical object that could exist in the real world.
tradition_approach: Extreme low-angle rakurs framing, strong diagonal composition, high-contrast black and white, graphic silhouettes against sky — the body as purposeful instrument against vast elemental forces
